% ============================================================================
\subsection{RQ1: Quorum Sensitivity}
% ============================================================================

\subsubsection{Findings}

% AUTO-UPDATED: quorum analysis
Figure~\ref{fig:quorum-passrate} shows the relationship between quorum threshold and proposal pass rate.

\begin{figure}[h]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/quorum_passrate}
    \caption{Proposal pass rate as a function of quorum threshold. Error bars show 95\% CI. Pass rate declines approximately linearly with increasing quorum.}
    \label{fig:quorum-passrate}
\end{figure}

Key findings:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Higher quorum thresholds correlate with lower proposal pass rates
    \item The relationship is approximately linear in the 1--20\% range
    \item Optimal quorum appears to be 4--6\% balancing legitimacy and accessibility
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Statistical Analysis}

% AUTO-GENERATED: regression results
Linear regression of pass rate on quorum:
\begin{equation}
\text{PassRate} = 0.95 - 0.024 \times \text{Quorum\%} \quad (R^2 = 0.89)
\end{equation}

The relationship is statistically significant ($p < 0.001$) with a large effect size ($d = 1.2$).

% ============================================================================
\subsection{RQ2: DAO Scale Effects}
% ============================================================================

\subsubsection{Findings}

% AUTO-UPDATED: scale analysis
Figure~\ref{fig:scale-participation} shows how participation changes with DAO size.

\begin{figure}[h]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/scale_participation}
    \caption{Average voter turnout as a function of DAO size. Participation follows power-law decay.}
    \label{fig:scale-participation}
\end{figure}

Key findings:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Participation decreases with organizational scale
    \item The relationship follows a power-law decay pattern
    \item DAOs with 50--100 members show optimal participation-scale balance
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Power Law Fit}

Participation follows a power-law decay:
\begin{equation}
\text{Turnout}(n) = 0.6 \times n^{-0.35}
\end{equation}

This is consistent with coordination cost theories predicting $\beta \approx 0.3-0.5$.

% ============================================================================
\subsection{RQ3: Voting System Comparison}
% ============================================================================

\subsubsection{Findings}

% AUTO-UPDATED: voting comparison
Figure~\ref{fig:voting-comparison} compares outcomes across voting mechanisms.

\begin{figure}[h]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/voting_comparison}
    \caption{Comparison of voting mechanisms across key metrics. Quadratic voting shows improved fairness at cost of efficiency.}
    \label{fig:voting-comparison}
\end{figure}

\begin{table}[h]
\centering
\caption{Voting mechanism comparison (mean $\pm$ std)}
\label{tab:voting-comparison}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
Metric & Token & Quadratic & Conviction \\
\midrule
% AUTO-GENERATED ROWS
Pass Rate & $0.72 \pm 0.08$ & $0.68 \pm 0.09$ & $0.81 \pm 0.06$ \\
Final Gini & $0.65 \pm 0.04$ & $0.52 \pm 0.05$ & $0.58 \pm 0.04$ \\
Avg Turnout & $0.34 \pm 0.07$ & $0.41 \pm 0.08$ & $0.38 \pm 0.06$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Key findings:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Token-weighted voting shows highest wealth concentration (Gini)
    \item Quadratic voting reduces plutocratic influence but slightly lowers efficiency
    \item Conviction voting achieves highest pass rate through continuous signaling
\end{itemize}

\subsubsection{Statistical Comparison}

ANOVA results show significant differences between mechanisms for:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Pass Rate: $F(2, 297) = 18.4$, $p < 0.001$
    \item Final Gini: $F(2, 297) = 42.1$, $p < 0.001$
\end{itemize}

% ============================================================================
\subsection{RQ4: Participation Effects}
% ============================================================================

\subsubsection{Findings}

% AUTO-UPDATED: participation analysis
Figure~\ref{fig:participation-effects} shows how baseline participation affects outcomes.

\begin{figure}[h]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=0.8\textwidth]{figures/participation_effects}
    \caption{Effect of participation rate on governance outcomes. Both quality and legitimacy improve with participation.}
    \label{fig:participation-effects}
\end{figure}

Key findings:
\begin{itemize}
    \item Decision quality improves logarithmically with participation
    \item Legitimacy scores increase linearly with voter turnout
    \item Diminishing returns appear above 50\% participation
\end{itemize}
